need ai pre-commit "make code easily consumable so code reads like a book and is skimmable"

only bottleneck is consuming code, so make sure to tell codex that you want just that:

"write extremely easy to consume code, optimize for how easy the code is to read. make the code skimmable.  avoid cleverness. use early returns."

also run this as a standalone prompt in ADDITION to adding it to ~/.codex/AGENTS.md

it should be like a pre-commit lmao. ai coding works 100x better when you give it an explicit focus to make code simple, much worse as only a background instruction

i find great success with standalone "cleanup prompts" to my prs. you can stuff every rule you got into agents.md, but beautiful code is secondary until it's the only focus

for example: "simplify all code so it's extremely easy to consume, remove not strictly necessary code"

reduce the number of possible states a function can be in with distriminated unions, remove any optionality that is not actual optional, never pass params for overriding code except strictly necessary

We’re introducing GPT-5.4 mini and nano, our most capable small models yet.

GPT-5.4 mini is more than 2x faster than GPT-5 mini. Optimized for coding, computer use, multimodal understanding, and subagents.

For lighter-weight tasks, GPT-5.4 nano is our smallest and cheapest
GPT-5.4 mini approaches the performance of the larger GPT-5.4 model on several evaluations, including SWE-Bench Pro and OSWorld-Verified.

GPT-5.4 mini is available today in the API, Codex, and ChatGPT.

In the API, it has a 400k context window. In Codex, it uses only 30% of the GPT-5.4 quota, letting you handle simpler coding tasks for about one-third of the cost.

GPT-5.4 nano is only available in the API.

GPT-5.4 mini is available today in ChatGPT, Codex, and the API.

Optimized for coding, computer use, multimodal understanding, and subagents. And it’s 2x faster than GPT-5 mini.

It's lovely to have Codex and Claude Code look at each other's worktrees on my project.  They're considerate and constructive, and never show any jealousy about me cheating on them with their main competitor.  I feel the result is better than the sum of its parts.

Claude Code sometimes gets confused though: Claude told me not to cherry-pick because 'the branch is already deleted.' It wasn't. The branch still existed locally and on origin, the commit was in the log, and cherry-pick was the cleanest solution.

I like how opinionated Clause is on design decisions, though, but I prefer how much more terse Codex is. Claude seems overly cautions, asking for permission to run safe commands. Codex just gets on with it.
